prompt:
A highly detailed 19th-century naturalist scientific illustration of a mountainous landscape, drawn in the style of Charles Darwin-era field notebooks (mid-1800s).

The scene shows a large valley surrounded by hills and ridges, rendered with fine ink linework and delicate watercolor washes.

Multiple faint walking paths descend from different points along the hills toward the same valley floor. Each path follows the natural slope of the terrain, curving and zig-zagging realistically as if following steep gradients.

Some paths are direct and smooth, others are winding and indirect, but all consistently move downhill and converge toward a lush, fertile basin at the bottom.

The terrain is rendered with cross-hatching, contour-like shading, and botanical detailing typical of 19th-century naturalist illustrations.

Foreground includes small plants, rocks, and soil textures carefully observed and drawn.

Color palette: muted earth tones, sepia ink, pale green washes, parchment background.

Composition: single plate illustration, no modern perspective distortion, slightly flattened scientific perspective.

NO text, NO labels, NO numbers, NO diagrams.

Style constraints: hand-drawn ink lines, visible pen strokes, subtle watercolor fills, aged paper texture, naturalist observation style.

Negative prompt: no modern graphics, no charts, no axes, no digital rendering, no neon colors, no futuristic elements, no typography
tradition_approach: Used fine ink linework, cross-hatching, muted watercolor washes, and observational terrain studies typical of Darwin-era illustrations to depict iterative downhill paths converging to a valley.
